-- 2 of 19 --

My Journey Into Storytelling
Matthew Dicks is the internationally bestselling author of the novels
Memoirs of an Imaginary Friend, Something Missing, Unexpectedly,
Milo, The Perfect Comeback of Caroline Jacobs, Twenty-one Truths
About Love, and The Other Mother, and the nonfiction titles
Storyworthy: Engage, Teach, Persuade, and Change Your Life through
the Power of Storytelling and Someday Is Today: 22 Simple Actionable
Ways to Propel Your Creative Life.
His novels have been translated into more than 25 languages
worldwide.
Matthew is a record 59-time Moth StorySLAM champion and 9-time
GrandSLAM champion who tells stories on stages all over the world.
Matthew’s clients have included Amazon, Slack, Johnson & Johnson,
Saatchi & Saatchi, Salesforce, MetLife, Pfizer, The Freeman Companies,
Rustic Pathways, and many more.
Matthew is the creator, producer, and co-host of Speak Up Storytelling,
a podcast that teaches people to find and tell their best stories.

-- 11 of 19 --

There is nothing more important in this course than Homework for Life.
It is the foundation upon which you will begin finding stories, seeing stories in your
everyday life, and discovering how infinitesimal but unforgettable a story can be.
Seeing and telling stories from your everyday life will change the way you communicate
and connect with others and understand the breadth and depth of your life.
It is the difference between being a storyteller with a handful of great stories and a
storyteller who always has something new, entertaining, and engaging to say.
Homework for Life
The best storytellers can answer questions, provide examples, construct arguments,
convince others, and support assertions through stories.
Even better, storytellers use stories to connect with other human beings, foster and
strengthen relationships, and make sense of their life
The more stories you have, the more successful you will be, so finding stories is
crucial. Let me tell you how.
Daily Practice #1 - Homework for Life
https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/11b
OpE1khsuClJGi3zvpRDHuEIcj6k4AIqxzfN-
fUZCM/edit?usp=sharing
The Best Storytellers Have
the Most Stories to Tell
I’ve removed entries for privacy reasons, but otherwise, this is completely authentic.
As you can see, I tend to have more than one entry for a day, and it’s not simply things
that happened. Instead, Homework for Life entires often contain thoughts, words
spoken by me or others, and simple observations.
Here is a sample of my current Homework for Life entries.

-- 12 of 19 --

Here is the Homework for Life sample from the course and my book, Storyworthy:
Here is a Homework for Life sample from 2022. Please notice how after seven years, I've
gotten considerably better at seeing those meaningful, Homework for Life moments in my
daily life. I hope to keep getting better. You will, too, if you commit to the practice. I promise.
Storytelling for Parents Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 13 of 19 --

Did someone say something today that made you laugh? Cry? Become angry? Feel
confused? Feel frightened? Think differently?
Did anyone’s words make you see them or yourself or something else in a brand new light?
Did you do something today that you’ve never done before? Do something in a way you’ve
never done it before?
Did you watch someone do something you’ve never done before?
Did you fail at something today? Succeed at something? Come agonizingly close to success?
Did you see something that you’ve never seen before? Rarely seen? Did you see something
that you see almost every day but in a new light today?
Did you think a new thought today? Ask yourself a question? Answer a question? Find the
wrong answer to a question?
Did a memorable “What if?” cross your mind today?
Did you lose hope today? Find hope? Wish for hope?
Did you say something today that you regret? Did you say something that you liked? Did you
want to say something but couldn’t find the courage to do so? Did you think of the perfect
thing to say after the fact?
Did you remember a moment from your past? Something you’d forgotten or rarely think
about? Did someone remind you of something from the past?
Did you do something that you would never tell anyone about? Did you think something that
you would never tell anyone about?
Did you do or think something that you wish you could tell someone about?
Did you keep a secret? Uncover a secret? Wish something had remained a secret?
Did something make you happy today? Sad? Embarrassed? Humiliated? Frightened?
Nervous? Less confident? More confident?
Did you want to punch someone today? Hug someone? Kiss someone?
Did you have the urge to do something today but fail to find the courage to do so?
Did you end the day with a regret?
WORKSHEET #3 - Homework for Life Prompts
Here are some prompts that might help:
Storytelling for Parents Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 14 of 19 --

First Last Best Worst
We move through our lives failing to see, tell, and record some of our most meaningful
stories. But those stories still exist, just waiting for us to find, tell, and record them.
First Last Best Worst is one way of finding the stories you've left behind and expanding
the view of your life as a whole.
WORKSHEET #4 - First Last Best Worst
SUBJECT 	FIRST 	LAST 	BEST 	WORST
Kiss
Teacher
Pet
Injury
Sport
Friend

THE FIRST LAST BEST WORST TEMPLATE
Storytelling for Parents Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 15 of 19 --

Where Oh Where
One of the best ways of finding a story from your past is to take a mental tour of the
locations of our life.
Stories hide in places, so when we return to those places, we can often find stories
that we have left behind. "Where Oh Where" teaches you how to do this.
Here is a list of locations to start. For each location, make a list of all the locations
where you have actually spent time in your life. All the zoos, Ferris wheels, and
puddles that you can recall.
Be relentless in making this list. Think hard. Consider every period of your life. Take
some time to ensure the list is complete. The longer the list, the greater your chance
of finding stories.
Then place yourself in each of those places in your mind’s eye and see what you can
see, hear, and remember. If you give yourself a chance, there is a good possibility
that you will remember something previously forgotten.
Where Oh Where Locations
Locker room
Concert hall
Swimming pool
Foyer
Furniture store
Christmas tree farm
Parking lot
Gas station
Laundromat
Puddle
School nurse’s office
Train station
Dentist office
Church pew
Tent
Snow fort
Museum
Locker room
Mountain summit
Public restroom
Tidal pool
Bathtub
Dressing room
Intersection
Nursing home
Arcade
Treehouse
Flower garden
Backyard
Waiting room
Post office
Hole
Ambulance
Basement
Funeral home
Tennis court
Gazebo
Under the covers
Driveway
High school dance
Mall
Cemetery
High school science lab
Pizza place
Leaf pile
Ferris wheel
Stairwell
Drive-in movie theater
Police car
Zoo
Front lawn
Add your own personal locations. Use locations that are familiar to your family.
Frequent haunts, favorite restaurants, local parks, vacation spots, and even
specific locations in the home. My list can help you get started, but specificity can
drive your storytelling.
Storytelling for Parents Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks

-- 16 of 19 --

3-2-1
Choose 3 tangible, non-proper nouns. You can use the online generator or ask for
prompts from the people playing with you (thus the #3).
Choose one of those prompts. Tell a story from your life that includes that
chosen prompt. The story can be as short as 20 seconds or as long as 2 minutes
(thus the #2), and quality does not matter. Finding stories matters.
You have 1 minute to prepare the story before telling (thus the #1).
1.
2.
3.
The purpose of this exercise is not to tell an outstanding story but to find new stories.
This is why you only have one minute to prepare.
Low stakes. No expectations. Just find stories.
Grommet
Balaclava
Plinth
3-2-1 is a storytelling improv game that invites you to look through your life through
the lens of a single object. Oftentimes a pinpoint, laser-pointer-like prompt through
your life can yield stories previously forgotten.
3-2-1 also affords you the opportunity to practice the storytelling strategies in a live,
no-stakes setting.
It’s also an excellent game to play at the beginning of a meeting or as a break in a
conference setting. It affords your people the opportunity to tell stories absent any
expectations and stakes while promoting greater connection and trust in teams.
You can use this online object generator (my favorite) to produce the three words
that you will use for your prompts.
https://perchance.org/object
You can use an AI tool like ChatGPT to assist and make your nouns more specific. I
often use this as my AI prompt to challenge myself:
"Make a list of three tangible, non-proper, obscure nouns."
But be warned. My most recent list was:
1.
2.
3.
I didn't actually know what a plinth even was! This raised the stakes considerably for
me, but that is what I wanted. It's not advised. And I had a balaclava story!
But AI can help you find words that children are more likely to know, words from
certain time periods, and ideas I have yet to even consider.
Rules for 3-2-1
Storytelling for Parents Workbook © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks
